summary: Joel Spolsky explains why Fog Creek chose Visual Basic for CityDesk's GUI development, arguing that top-notch C++ programmers become extraordinarily productive when working in VB. He divides programmers into three tiers based on their ability to handle pointers, then argues that the single most impactful language feature for productivity is managed memory, not OOP or any other paradigm. He acknowledges VB's limitations but notes the ability to drop into C++ for performance-critical code makes it a practical choice. He predicts C# will eventually succeed VB in the Windows GUI space, while dismissing Java for desktop apps due to its non-native feel.
key_points: Programmers fall into three groups: those who can't program, those who can but not with pointers, and the elite few who handle pointers and indirection naturally.
The only language feature that genuinely improves productivity is managed memory (garbage collection or reference counting), not OOP, UML, Java, or any other fad.
Expert C++ programmers writing in VB become 'super-efficient coding machines' producing clean, object-oriented code without wasting time on low-level plumbing.
VB's ability to drop into C/C++ for performance-critical inner loops provides an escape hatch that makes it viable for commercial applications.
Java's GUI libraries produce applications that look and feel non-native, making it unsuitable for desktop software where user experience is the competitive advantage.
money_quotes: For some reason, some people just do not seem to be able to write code with pointers in it. They were born without the part of the brain that does indirection, I guess.

What I wondered was, what happens if you take top-notch C++ programmers who dream in pointers, and let them code in VB. What I discovered at Fog Creek was that they become super-efficient coding machines.
The only thing that improves your programming productivity is using managed code – that is, using a language in which memory management is automatic.
I don't care how you do it, just let me concatenate strings without thinking about where the new bigger string will go and I'll be happy.
And the worst part about coding in VB is that people think you're not cool because your code doesn't have {'s and }'s. I can live with the shame if it means I'm more productive.
Every Java app LOOKS like a Java app, takes forever to launch, and just doesn't feel completely native.
At some point, you have to stop debating and write code!
key_insight: The biggest productivity gain in programming comes not from paradigms or methodologies but from automatic memory management, and skilled programmers should use the highest-level tool that gets the job done rather than chasing language prestige.
